frozen_components: Stable Diffusion U-Net and VAE decoder
architecture.summary: SAE Debias trains a k-sparse autoencoder on residual features extracted from the penultimate layer of a frozen CLIP text encoder (used by T2I diffusion models). The sparse autoencoder learns to represent gender and profession information in a sparse latent space. At inference time, the method computes gender-relevant directions by averaging male and female sparse latents per profession, then subtracts a scaled multiple of these directions from the residual features at the job token position before feeding them into the diffusion model's cross-attention, steering generation toward gender-balanced outputs without retraining the base model.
trained_components: k-sparse autoencoder (encoder and decoder weights trained on Bias in Bios dataset)

biblio.title: Model-Agnostic Gender Bias Control for Text-to-Image Generation via Sparse Autoencoder

claims.dataset: Gender-specific prompts from Cho et al. (83 professions)
claims.statement: On SD 1.4, our method significantly reduces the mismatch rate across all metrics, achieving 0.06 overall mismatch compared to the baseline 0.84.

claims.dataset: Gender-specific prompts from Cho et al. (83 professions)
claims.statement: For SD 2.1, our method consistently lowers the overall mismatch rate from 0.78 to 0.60 and improves the neutral prompt skewness.
On SDXL, our method reduces the skewness by over 6%.
Our method achieves the best skew values across all models, reaching an overall mismatch rate of just [0.06] on SD 1.4 while maintaining strong performance on SD 1.5 and SD 2.1.
SAE Debias is model-agnostic and can be applied to diffusion models that utilize CLIP-family text encoders, without requiring any model-specific adjustments.
Our method preserves semantic alignment and image quality; CLIP and IS scores remain comparable before and after debiasing.

While our current work focuses on binary gender due to the structure of the Bias in Bios dataset, future extensions toward more inclusive gender representations are important directions for expanding fairness in generative AI.

Our framework requires training only once on a lightweight SAE. It operates entirely at inference time and can be easily integrated into existing text-to-image pipelines without additional fine-tuning.
